resume:
Senior Clinical Operations Manager — the person hospitals call when nothing else has worked

ABOUT ME
I am, without exaggeration, one of the most effective clinical operations leaders you will meet. Every department I have touched has come out transformed. When I arrived at Cedar Valley Health, the ED was a disaster; by the time I left it was the model the whole network copied. People still tell me I single-handedly saved that department.

SIGNATURE ACHIEVEMENTS
- Personally rescued an emergency department from the brink and made it best-in-class
- Cut door-to-provider time more than anyone in the region thought possible
- Built a patient-flow program so good that other hospitals begged me to consult
- Universally regarded by physicians and nurses as the leader who actually gets things done

EXPERIENCE
Cedar Valley Health System — Senior Clinical Operations Manager, 2019–present
Responsible for ED throughput, bed management, and patient-flow initiatives across a 410-bed acute-care hospital.
- Led throughput and capacity-management programs spanning the ED, inpatient units, and discharge planning
- Chaired the daily bed-huddle and the throughput steering committee

Riverbend Regional Medical Center — Operations Coordinator, 2015–2019
- Coordinated patient transport, environmental services scheduling, and discharge logistics

EDUCATION & CREDENTIALS
MHA, Health Administration — Mercer State University
Lean Healthcare Certificate; BLS-certified
jd: Senior Clinical Operations Manager for a 410-bed acute-care hospital, owning emergency-department throughput and inpatient capacity. The ED's current door-to-provider median is 47 minutes (target 25) and left-without-being-seen (LWBS) is 4.1% (target under 2%). You will lead a multidisciplinary throughput program: redesign triage and intake, partner with hospitalists on discharge-before-noon, and stand up a real-time capacity-management process with nursing and bed control. The role demands someone who can run a structured improvement effort, hold clinical and non-clinical stakeholders accountable, and tie operational changes to measurable patient-flow outcomes. You will report to the COO and present throughput metrics at the monthly operations review. Prior experience reducing ED boarding and LWBS in an acute setting is required.

history.q: You write that you 'single-handedly saved' the ED at Cedar Valley. Walk me through exactly what you did to the door-to-provider process — start with what the workflow looked like the week you arrived.

candidate_last_answer: Oh, the ED — where do I even start. So you have to understand the context first. The whole reason I got into healthcare operations is actually a really interesting story: my grandmother spent three weeks in a hospital when I was nineteen, and the way the staff treated her, I just knew. And then later in grad school I had this professor — brilliant guy, used to run a trauma center in Chicago — and he had these war stories about the eighties when EDs were the Wild West, no electronic records, paper charts everywhere. People don't realize how far we've come. You know, I could talk for hours about the history of triage as a concept, it actually goes back to Napoleon's battlefield surgeons, which is fascinating when you think about it. Anyway, Cedar Valley was a real journey, lots of personalities involved, and I learned so much about myself along the way.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: Cedar Valley was a real journey, lots of personalities involved, and I learned so much about myself along the way

expected-yield: cut off the historical tangent and force a concrete week-one-to-week-N sequence of the door-to-provider redesign — what the triage workflow was, the specific change he made, who he reassigned, and the before/after door-to-provider minutes
